desc: Medically significant — the only dangerous spider regularly encountered in LA County. Glossy black with red hourglass on ventral abdomen. Webs in dark, undisturbed areas.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅓ in–⅔ in body. ♂: much smaller.
fm.Web: Irregular, strong cobweb in sheltered dark areas
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes; poor vision; relies on vibrations
fm.Habitat: Under rocks, woodpiles, outdoor structures, garages
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; shy; bites only when trapped against skin
fm.Season: Year-round; males wander seeking mates in spring–fall

desc: Invasive — arrived in SoCal ~2000s, now abundant. Tan-brown with geometric pattern. Spiky egg sac diagnostic. Less venomous than Black Widow but rapidly displacing it.

fm.Web: Irregular cobweb under outdoor furniture, eaves, cars
fm.Habitat: Urban — under patio furniture, mailboxes, playground equipment
fm.Behavior: Less aggressive than Black Widow; frequently encountered

desc: Often confused with Black Widow. Dark with bulbous abdomen but lacks red hourglass. Essentially harmless.

fm.Web: Cobweb in dark corners, garages, basements
fm.Behavior: Shy; bite causes mild, brief pain only

desc: Largest and most conspicuous jumping spider in LA. Bright red abdomen contrasts with black cephalothorax. Active visual hunter with charismatic behavior.

fm.Web: No hunting web; silken retreat for resting/molting
fm.Habitat: Open sunny areas, fences, walls, garden structures
fm.Behavior: Active diurnal hunter; curious; turns to face observers

desc: Black with white spots and iridescent green chelicerae. One of the most commonly encountered jumping spiders.

fm.Eyes: 4 large anterior eyes; excellent binocular vision
fm.Habitat: Gardens, buildings, fences — any sunny surface
fm.Behavior: Active hunter; stalks prey with cat-like approach

desc: Introduced tropical species now abundant in urban LA. ♂ distinctive with white lateral stripes on black body.

fm.Habitat: Buildings, walls, window frames; strongly synanthropic
fm.Behavior: Active diurnal hunter; very fast

desc: Large, spectacular orb weaver. Female builds web up to 2 feet across with distinctive zigzag stabilimentum in center.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅔ in–1 in body (very large). ♂: ⅛ in–⅓ in.
fm.Web: Large circular orb with thick zigzag stabilimentum
fm.Habitat: Gardens, meadows, tall vegetation in sunny areas
fm.Behavior: Sits head-down in web center; bounces web to capture prey

desc: Silvery orb weaver with lobed abdomen. Web has stabilimentum. Common in warm SoCal gardens.

fm.Web: Orb web with X or cross-shaped stabilimentum
fm.Habitat: Gardens, warm dry areas, coastal scrub
fm.Behavior: Sits in web center with legs in X-shape

desc: The most common large orb weaver in LA gardens. Builds new web nightly in the same location. Variable coloring.

fm.Web: Vertical orb web; rebuilt nightly; sits in center at night
fm.Habitat: Gardens, under eaves, near outdoor lights (attracts prey)
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal web builder; hides during day; attracted to lights

desc: Large, fast spider frequently found indoors. Builds funnel-shaped sheet web. Males wander seeking females in fall.

fm.Size: ♀: ½ in–⅔ in body; leg span up to 3 in
fm.Web: Flat sheet web with funnel retreat in corners
fm.Habitat: Indoors — garages, basements, closets, window wells
fm.Behavior: Fast runner when disturbed; males wander in fall

desc: Large, flat-bodied spider with legs spread crab-like. Nocturnal ambush predator on walls and ceilings. Harmless.

fm.Size: ♀: ⅔ in–¾ in body; very large leg span
fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, rocky areas; sometimes enters homes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flat against surfaces; extremely fast

desc: One of the largest wolf spiders. Females carry egg sac on spinnerets, then carry spiderlings on back. Ground-dwelling nocturnal hunter.

fm.Web: No web; digs shallow burrow or roams
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes; 2 large posterior median eyes reflective at night
fm.Habitat: Open ground, gardens, under debris; burrows in soil
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; active hunter; ♀ carries egg sac then spiderlings on back

desc: Ambush predator that changes color between white and yellow to match flower color. Catches pollinators on blooms.

fm.Web: No web; ambush predator on flowers
fm.Habitat: On flowers — especially composites, roses, buckwheat
fm.Behavior: Color-changing ambush predator; catches bees and butterflies on flowers

desc: The "daddy long-legs" of buildings. Extremely common indoors. Vibrates web rapidly when disturbed.

fm.Size: ♀: ¼ in–⅓ in body; very long legs (1–2 in)
fm.Web: Loose irregular web in corners, ceilings, garages
fm.Eyes: 8 eyes in two groups of three + two
fm.Behavior: Vibrates rapidly when disturbed ("vibrating spider"); preys on other spiders

desc: Introduced. Brick-red cephalothorax and large forward-pointing fangs specialized for piercing isopod (sowbug) armor. Found under logs and bark.

fm.Web: Silken retreat under bark/logs; no prey web
fm.Habitat: Under bark, logs, rocks, leaf litter — wherever sowbugs live
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal sowbug specialist; large fangs pierce isopod armor

desc: LA County's native tarantula. Males wander in fall seeking mates — the famous "tarantula migration." Docile, long-lived (♀ 20+ years). Burrow dweller.

fm.Size: ♀: body 1.5–2 in; leg span up to 5 in
fm.Web: Lines burrow with silk; no prey web
fm.Eyes: 8 tiny eyes; poor vision; relies on vibration
fm.Habitat: Chaparral, grasslands, oak woodland; hillside burrows
fm.Behavior: ♂ wander Sep–Nov seeking ♀; docile; flicks urticating hairs if harassed

desc: Native to SoCal deserts — the only recluse spider established in the LA region. Medically significant bite (necrotic). Violin marking on cephalothorax; 6 eyes (not 8).

fm.Web: Irregular sheet web in dark recesses
fm.Eyes: 6 eyes in 3 pairs (diagnostic — most spiders have 8)
fm.Habitat: Desert and semi-arid areas; rock crevices; occasionally buildings
fm.Behavior: Extremely shy; nocturnal; bites only when pressed against skin

desc: Native SoCal trapdoor spider. Builds silk-lined burrow with hinged lid camouflaged with debris. Ambush predator.

fm.Web: Silk-lined burrow with hinged trapdoor lid
fm.Behavior: Sits in burrow, lid slightly open; lunges at passing prey

desc: The largest scorpion in North America — up to 6 in. Desert-edge LA County. Fluoresces brilliant green under UV light. Sting painful but not medically significant.

fm.Habitat: Desert scrub, sandy washes — northeast LA County
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; ambush predator; fluoresces green under UV

desc: The most commonly encountered scorpion in residential SoCal. Small, tan, found under rocks and in homes. Mild sting.

fm.Habitat: Under rocks, bark, debris; enters homes; gardens
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in moist dark areas; UV-detectable

desc: Bright green ambush spider on flowers. ♀ spits venom.

fm.Behavior: Ambush; ♀ guards egg sac; spits venom

desc: Introduced. White cross on abdomen.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal builder

desc: Silver/dark banded. Orb in grass.

fm.Behavior: Web center; vibrates when threatened

desc: Pale yellow-green. Enters homes. Mild bite.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in sac by day

desc: Dark, fast. White dorsal "collar" mark.

fm.Behavior: Fast nocturnal runner

desc: Introduced. Marbled abdomen.

fm.Behavior: Vibrates web

desc: Introduced. Similar to Desert Recluse. Occasionally found in imported goods/structures. 6 eyes.

fm.Web: Irregular sheet web in dark recesses
fm.Behavior: Very shy; nocturnal; medically significant bite (rare)

desc: Introduced. Dark with cream marking. Bite can cause inflammation. Expanding.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; bite mildly medically significant

desc: Flattened jumper on sun-warmed walls. Two white side stripes.

fm.Behavior: Hunts on warm walls; flattened for crack-hiding

desc: Spotted orbweaver — large rusty-brown orb weaver with cruciate (cross-shaped) dark mark on abdomen. Builds enormous webs on buildings at night. Very common urban spider.

fm.Color: Rusty brown with dark cross-mark on abdomen

desc: Red abdomen, black markings. CA endemic.

fm.Behavior: Active hunter; curious; red color distinctive

desc: Most common trail tick. Can transmit RMSF.

fm.Size: ⅛ in unfed; ½ in engorged
fm.Behavior: Quests on grass tips for passing hosts

desc: Grape jumping spider — tiny colorful jumper found on grape and other plants. ♂ with bright red-orange face and metallic green chelicerae. Elaborate courtship display.

fm.Color: ♂ red face, green chelicerae; ♀ brown striped

desc: Colorful jumper — ♂ red face + green chelicerae in courtship dance.

fm.Behavior: ♂ elaborate courtship dance with colors; studied for vision

desc: Most common LA scorpion. Small tan. Mild sting. Fluoresces UV.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; glows under UV; mild sting

desc: Most common LA tarantula. Males cross roads in fall.

fm.Behavior: ♂ wander roads Oct–Nov; docile; urticating hairs

desc: Lyme disease vector. Smaller than Pacific Coast Tick.

fm.Behavior: Transmits Lyme disease; nymphs highest risk spring

desc: Introduced. Fast dark spider with fuzzy silk. Very common.

fm.Behavior: Very fast; fuzzy bluish web; harmless

desc: Burrowing scorpion. Thick tail.

fm.Behavior: Burrows in hard soil; thick tail; mild sting; UV fluorescent

desc: Introduced Australian. Lace-web on window frames.

fm.Behavior: Builds lace-web; very common on buildings

desc: Small dark jumping spider. Very common on buildings and fences.

fm.Behavior: Active hunter; common urban jumper

desc: Color-changing ambush spider on flowers. White or yellow to match flower.

fm.Behavior: Changes color white↔yellow to match flower; ambush predator

desc: Dark sac spider with distinctive broad head.

fm.Habitat: Under bark, leaf litter, enters homes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; broad cephalothorax distinctive

desc: Extraordinary hunting method — swings sticky bolas ball on silk thread to catch moths. Lures with moth pheromone mimicry.

fm.Web: No orb — swings single sticky silk bolas
fm.Habitat: Shrubs, gardens — where moths fly at night
fm.Behavior: Swings sticky silk ball at moths; mimics moth pheromones; unique hunting strategy

desc: The only scorpion in NA with potentially life-threatening sting. Climbs walls. Rare in LA County — desert edge.

fm.Habitat: Desert — under bark, in rock crevices; climbs walls/ceilings
fm.Behavior: Climbs vertical surfaces; bark-loving; most medically significant NA scorpion

desc: Bright orange-red jumping spider. Males with striking display. In chaparral and scrub.
Elongated spider with huge chelicerae. Builds orb webs near water. Males and females lock jaws during mating.

fm.Body: Elongated spider with huge chelicerae

desc: Triangulate cobweb spider — small house spider with distinctive zigzag triangular marks on abdomen. Extremely common indoors. Preys on other spiders including brown widows. Beneficial.
Bizarre spider with abdominal spines. Small orb web in woodland understory.

fm.Body: Bizarre spider with abdominal spines

desc: Green spider that hunts on vegetation. Does not build web. Ambushes prey with excellent vision.

fm.Body: Green spider that hunts on vegetation

desc: Golden-blonde tarantula of desert margins. Males wander in fall seeking mates.
Builds sheet webs with funnel retreat in grass and low vegetation. Very fast. Venom studied for neuroscience.

fm.Web: Funnel sheet web in grass, shrubs
fm.Behavior: Extremely fast — venom studied in neuroscience

desc: Slow spider that spits sticky venomous silk to immobilize prey from a distance. Six eyes.
Whip scorpion that sprays acetic acid (vinegar). Nocturnal desert predator. Harmless to humans.

fm.Venom: None — whip scorpion, not true scorpion

desc: Largest jumping spider in NA. Black with white spots and iridescent chelicerae. Popular in pet trade.

fm.Color: Black with white spots; iridescent green chelicerae

desc: Variable color orbweaver — yellow, orange, or marbled. Round abdomen. Fall webbuilder.

fm.Body: Variable color orbweaver — yellow, orange, or marbled

desc: Southern house spider — large dark spider in old buildings. ♂ slender with long palps; ♀ stocky. Males often mistaken for brown recluse. Builds messy webs in crevices. Harmless.

fm.Color: ♀ dark brown stocky; ♂ tan slender with long palps

desc: Long-legged spider in garages and cellars. Native species (unlike the European P. phalangioides).

fm.Web: Irregular web in cellars, dark spaces
fm.Behavior: Long-legged cellar spider

desc: Tiny black and white striped jumper on walls and windows. European introduction. Hunts flies.

fm.Behavior: Stalks flies on vertical surfaces

desc: Orbweaver with distinctive horn-like bumps on abdomen. Builds large webs on porches in fall.

fm.Color: Brown/gray with horn-like bumps on abdomen
fm.Web: Large orb web on porches, eaves

desc: Orange-red abdomen with black cephalothorax. Complex courtship dance. Found in meadows and gardens.
Two distinct male forms — tufted (black) and gray. Rare example of within-species dimorphism in spiders.

fm.Body: Two distinct male forms — tufted (black) and gray

desc: Translucent green jumping spider on leaves. Huge forward-facing eyes. Hunts small insects.

fm.Body: Translucent green jumping spider on leaves

desc: Small bronze-brown jumping spider on tree trunks and fences. White lateral bands. Very common.

fm.Body: Small bronze-brown jumping spider on tree trunks and fences

desc: Large orbweaver with round spotted abdomen. Variable color — white, tan, or pink. Fall webbuilder.

fm.Body: Large orbweaver with round spotted abdomen

desc: Small orbweaver with unique star-shaped spiny abdomen. Nocturnal webbuilder on shrubs.

fm.Body: Small orbweaver with unique star-shaped spiny abdomen

desc: Brown orbweaver. Builds large webs on buildings and porch lights. Nocturnal. Takes web down at dawn.

fm.Body: Brown orbweaver

desc: Pale yellow-green sac spider. Builds silk retreats in corners. Occasional biter — mild venom. European.
Small dark wolf spider abundant on ground in many habitats. Females carry egg sac and spiderlings.

fm.Behavior: ♀ carries egg sac on spinnerets

desc: Long-legged harvestman (daddy longlegs). Not a spider — single body segment, no venom, no silk.
Large tan solifuge of desert margins. Huge chelicerae. Nocturnal predator. Not venomous. Very fast.

fm.Habitat: Large tan solifuge of desert margins

desc: Tiny arachnid (<⅛ in) with scorpion-like pincers but no tail/stinger. Beneficial — eats mites, booklice.

fm.Behavior: Beneficial — eats booklice, dust mites

desc: Small dark cobweb spider. Common in buildings. European introduction. Harmless relative of widows.
Small orbweaver building incomplete web with open sector leading to retreat. On buildings year-round.

fm.Web: Incomplete orb with open sector + signal thread
fm.Behavior: Signal line leads to retreat — detects prey vibrations

desc: Tiny cribellate orbweaver. One of only two spider families worldwide that lack venom glands entirely.

fm.Behavior: No venom glands — wraps prey in silk only

desc: Dark ground spider of sac spider family. Found under rocks and debris in coastal sage scrub. Native to Baja and SoCal.

fm.Habitat: Under rocks, debris in coastal sage scrub

desc: Bright metallic green jumping spider with orange markings. Large for a jumper. On shrubs and low vegetation.

fm.Body: Bright metallic green jumping spider with orange markings

desc: Small pale jumping spider with white chelicerae. Common on bushes and flowers. Hunts small flies.

fm.Body: Small pale jumping spider with white chelicerae

desc: Iridescent green-bronze jumping spider. Ant-mimicking body shape. Found on low vegetation.

fm.Body: Iridescent green-bronze jumping spider

desc: Elongate ant-mimicking jumping spider. Lives near ant trails and preys on ants.

fm.Body: Elongate ant-mimicking jumping spider

desc: Black ground spider with white spots mimicking velvet ants. Nocturnal hunter under rocks.

fm.Body: Black ground spider with white spots mimicking velvet ants

desc: Mouse spider. Velvety gray-brown. Nocturnal hunter in houses. European introduction.
Tiny spider building distinctive bowl-shaped sheet web with flat doily beneath. In shrubs and gardens.
Small dome-web spider in shaded understory. Inverted under dome, catches insects falling onto web.

fm.Body: Small dome-web spider in shaded understory

desc: Small orbweaver with distinctive lattice-like abdominal pattern. Nocturnal webbuilder on fences and plants.

fm.Body: Small orbweaver with distinctive lattice-like abdominal pattern

desc: Large velvety red mite emerging after rain. Predatory on insect eggs. Beneficial garden inhabitant.
Tiny red-brown mite invading homes in spring. Feeds on clover and grass. Leaves red stains when crushed.
Brown bark-matching crab spider. Ambushes prey on tree trunks. Excellent camouflage on oak bark.

fm.Body: Brown bark-matching crab spider

desc: Dark brown crab spider active on ground and leaf litter. Ambushes prey. Common in natural areas.

fm.Body: Dark brown crab spider active on ground and leaf litter

desc: Very common cobweb spider in homes worldwide. Brown with mottled pattern. Beneficial pest controller.
California trapdoor spider relative. Builds silk-lined burrow without door. Large, dark. Endemic to California.

fm.Behavior: Long-lived mygalomorph; does not build trapdoor

desc: Primitive mygalomorph building turret-shaped collar around burrow entrance. Long-lived. California native.

fm.Behavior: Primitive mygalomorph — builds turret not trapdoor

desc: Builds sheet web with funnel retreat in shrubs. Very fast — darts out to grab prey on web surface.

fm.Body: Builds sheet web with funnel retreat in shrubs

desc: Dark spider mimicking carpenter ants. Waves front legs like antennae. Ground-dwelling predator.

fm.Body: Dark spider mimicking carpenter ants

desc: Dark spider building cribellate webs under bark and in crevices. Blue-white hackled silk.

fm.Body: Dark spider building cribellate webs under bark and in crevices

desc: Enters other spiders webs, plucks silk to lure prey. Specialist spider predator.

fm.Body: Enters other spiders webs, plucks silk to lure prey

desc: Medium brown spider endemic to CA. Often confused with brown recluse but harmless.

fm.Body: Medium brown spider endemic to CA

desc: Flat fast spider hunting on ground and bark. Long front legs. Common in natural areas.

fm.Body: Flat fast spider hunting on ground and bark

desc: Elongate dark spider mimicking large black ants. Waves front legs like antennae. Ground-dwelling.

fm.Body: Elongate dark spider mimicking large black ants

desc: Tiny dome-web spider of forest understory. Male fights on female's web. Important prey for birds.

fm.Behavior: Males fight on female webs

desc: Dark ground spider with white dorsal stripe (parson's cravat). Fast nocturnal hunter. Occasional biter.

fm.Body: Dark ground spider with white dorsal stripe (parson's cravat)

desc: Tiny dark jumping spider with two white spots on pedipalps waved like flags during courtship.

fm.Body: Tiny dark jumping spider with two white spots on pedipalps waved like flags during courtship

desc: Small orbweaver of coastal scrub and garden hedges. Variable brown-gray coloring.
Large orbweaver found in warmer areas. Hides in curled leaf during day, builds large web at night.
Sand-colored wolf spider camouflaged on beaches. Nocturnal hunter of beach invertebrates.
Pale green lynx spider hunting on shrubs. Ambushes prey with excellent vision and quick strike.
Tiny spider that decorates its web with a vertical line of debris and dead prey — 'trash line.' Spider hides in the line, perfectly camouflaged.

fm.Color: Brown; humped abdomen; sits in debris line

desc: Slender spider on vegetation. Active hunter — pounces on prey from leaves. Prominent leg spines. Important predator of crop pests.

fm.Color: Pale with dark stripes; prominent leg spines

desc: Small dark wolf spider extremely common in gardens. Runs on ground hunting prey. Mothers carry egg sacs, then spiderlings ride on back.
Harvestman with spiny body. Not a spider — single body section, no silk, no venom. Common under rocks and in leaf litter in mountains.

fm.Color: Brown; spiny body; very long legs

desc: Tiny spider building distinctive bowl-shaped web above a flat doily-shaped sheet. Very common in shrubs. Web architecture is diagnostic.

fm.Color: Dark with pale marks; distinctive bowl+doily web

desc: Large spider resembling wolf spider. Female carries egg sac in chelicerae (not on spinnerets). Builds nursery web tent for spiderlings.
Large flat fast spider. Not native — pantropical. Occasionally found in LA buildings. Harmless. Feared due to size but beneficial predator.
Medium brown spider recently established in California. Originally Mediterranean. Builds sheet webs. Can bite — mildly painful.

fm.Color: Brown with distinctive fiddle-like dorsal mark

desc: Bizarre spider that holds a small web between front legs and throws it over prey like a net. Enormous eyes for night hunting. Incredible behavior.

fm.Behavior: Holds expandable web between legs — throws it at prey

desc: Fast-moving solifuge (wind scorpion/camel spider). Not a spider — separate arachnid order. No venom. Enormous chelicerae. Nocturnal desert predator.

fm.Note: Not a spider — no venom, no silk

desc: Mule fat blister mite — tiny eriophyid mite causing conspicuous blister galls on mule fat (Baccharis) leaves. Galls are green-red raised bumps. Very common in riparian areas.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are raised blisters on mule fat leaves

desc: Koch's wolf spider — large ground-running wolf spider of open habitats. Nocturnal. Eyes reflect bright green with headlamp. Females carry egg sacs and young on their backs.

fm.Behavior: Eyes glow green with headlamp at night

desc: Ribbon jumping spider — dark jumping spider with distinctive white lateral stripes. Very common on tree bark and fences. Hunts actively by day. Often found on building exteriors.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with white lateral stripes

desc: Puritan scorpion — medium brown scorpion common in foothill and desert habitats. Found under rocks and bark. Mildly venomous — sting comparable to bee sting. Glows under UV light.

fm.Color: Brown; fluoresces blue-green under UV light
fm.Warning: Mild sting — comparable to bee sting

desc: Ghost spider — pale fast-running spider active at night. Builds no web — hunts by running down prey on foliage. Often found on walls at night near porch lights.
Trashline orbweaver — tiny spider decorating its web with a vertical line of debris. Spider sits motionless in the debris line, invisible to both predators and prey.

fm.Color: Brown; humped abdomen; hides in debris line

desc: Pacific orbweaver — medium brown orb weaver building webs in coastal scrub and chaparral. Nocturnal — takes down web at dawn. Common but secretive.

fm.Color: Brown with subtle markings; nocturnal web builder

desc: Jewel spider — large orb weaver with gem-like tubercles on abdomen. Builds enormous webs on buildings at night. Very common in suburban LA. Harmless.

cn: California Black Walnut Pouch Gall Mite
desc: Willow gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite inducing conspicuous raised bead-like galls on willow leaves. Very common along riparian corridors. Galls are diagnostic.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are raised beads on willow leaves

desc: Sagebrush gall mite — microscopic eriophyid mite causing enlarged distorted buds on sagebrush (Artemisia). Common in coastal sage scrub. Galls are swollen fuzzy buds.

fm.Color: Mite invisible; galls are swollen buds on sagebrush

desc: Foothill scorpion — medium brown scorpion of LA County foothills and canyons. Found under rocks and bark. Fluorescent blue-green under UV light. Mild sting.
Large spotted orbweaver — builds huge orb webs at night, takes them down by dawn. Hides in curled leaf during day. Distinctly spotted abdomen. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Brown with pale spots; nocturnal web builder

desc: Gertsch's sawfinger scorpion — small scorpion with distinctively serrated pedipalp fingers. Desert and foothill habitats. Nocturnal. Found under rocks. Mild sting.
Wall spider — tiny spider building small flat star-shaped webs on walls and ceilings. Extremely common on building exteriors worldwide. Often near light fixtures where it catches tiny insects.

fm.Color: Pale gray; flat star-shaped web on walls

desc: Superstition Mountains scorpion — dark robust scorpion. Found in rocky foothill habitats. Nocturnal. One of the larger scorpions in LA County mountains.
Catalina Island pseudoscorpion — tiny predatory arachnid resembling a scorpion without a tail. Lives in leaf litter. Grasps prey with pincers. Beneficial predator of mites and springtails.

fm.Color: Red-brown; scorpion-like pincers but no tail

desc: Graceful ground spider — small fast-running spider recently established in SoCal. Pantropical introduction. Found under debris in gardens. Builds no web.
Los Angeles scorpion — small-medium scorpion found in foothill canyons of the San Gabriel and Santa Monica Mountains. Nocturnal. Under rocks. Glows under UV.
American dog tick — medium-sized tick; major vector of Rocky Mountain spotted fever. Found in grassy and shrubby areas. Attaches to dogs, humans, and wildlife.

fm.Size: ⅛ in unfed; ½ in engorged
fm.Warning: Disease vector — check for ticks after hiking

desc: Pacific Coast tick — common tick of chaparral and grassland. Primary vector of 364D rickettsiosis in California. Attaches to deer, dogs, and humans.

fm.Color: Brown with ornate pale dorsal markings

desc: Minute jumping spider — extremely small jumper. Among the tiniest jumping spiders in LA County. On vegetation. Active hunter despite microscopic size.
California trapdoor spider — large primitive spider building silk-lined burrows with trapdoor. Massive chelicerae. Nocturnal. Foothill and mountain habitats. California endemic.
California paradise spider — tiny jumping spider — males perform elaborate courtship dances with colorful displays. Complex choreography studied by behavioral biologists.

fm.Color: ♂ colorful face+legs for courtship; ♀ cryptic brown

desc: McCook's wolf spider — medium wolf spider with tufted front legs used in courtship signaling. Males drum and wave leg tufts to females. Common in leaf litter.

fm.Color: Brown with pale median stripe; ♂ tufted front legs

desc: Palp-waving jumping spider — small gray jumping spider on building walls. Males wave enlarged palps during courtship. Very common on exterior walls and fences.

fm.Color: Gray with white markings; on walls

desc: Bordered ant-mimic spider — small ground spider that remarkably mimics ants in body shape and movement. Walks with front legs raised like antennae. On ground among ants.

fm.Color: Dark; ant-shaped body; mimics ant movement

desc: Willow eriophyid mite — microscopic mite causing curled and distorted willow leaves. Very common on willows along streams. Galls are diagnostic.
Long-palped lynx spider — bright green lynx spider similar to P. viridans but with longer palps. Ambush predator on flowers. Mothers guard egg sacs.

fm.Color: Bright green; spiny legs; long palps

desc: Gray lynx spider — small gray lynx spider on vegetation. Active predator. Prominent leg spines. Less common than striped and green lynx spiders.
Six-spotted orbweaver — tiny bright green orb weaver with six black spots on underside. Builds small orbs in shrubs. Very common but easily overlooked.
Arabesque orbweaver — medium orb weaver with intricate abdominal pattern. Builds web at dusk, removes at dawn. Common in gardens and woodland edges.
Athena's ant-mimic — dark ground spider remarkably resembling a large carpenter ant. Runs with ant-like gait. Predatory. Found among ants.
Dewdrop spider — tiny silver spider living as kleptoparasite in larger spiders' webs. Steals prey from host's web. Globular silver abdomen reflects light.
